name: Hieros Gamos
shortDescription: The Hieros Gamos, or sacred marriage, is a ritual or mythological union between a god and a goddess, often symbolizing fertility, cosmic harmony, and the renewal of the world.
description: The Hieros Gamos, meaning "sacred marriage" or "holy marriage" in Greek, is a significant concept in Babylonian mythology. It refers to the ritualized or mythological sexual union between a divine male and female, typically representing a god and a goddess. This union was not merely a symbolic act; it was believed to have profound consequences for the fertility of the land, the prosperity of the people, and the overall cosmic order. In the Babylonian context, the Hieros Gamos was prominently associated with the New Year festival (Akitu) and was often enacted between the king, representing the god (often Marduk), and a priestess, representing the goddess (often Ishtar or another fertility deity). The act was believed to ensure the continuation of life and the cyclical renewal of the world. The Enuma Elish, the Babylonian creation epic, hints at the cosmic implications of such unions, where the primordial waters (Apsu and Tiamat) represent the initial chaos that is overcome through the creation and ordering of the cosmos, potentially reflecting an early form of the Hieros Gamos concept. The ritual served to legitimize the king's rule and connect him directly to the divine realm, reinforcing the concept of sacred kingship. The absence of a successful Hieros Gamos could be interpreted as a sign of divine displeasure and portend disaster for the kingdom.

sources.title: Before Philosophy
sources.author: Henri Frankfort, H.A. Frankfort, John A. Wilson, Thorkild Jacobsen

sources.title: The Religion of Babylonia and Assyria
sources.author: Theophilus G. Pinches
